answers.field_label: Coach Supervision Related Training Qualifications And Certifications Including The Date Duration And Level
answers.value:
1. ICF Accredited Coach Training Program (ACTP) – Professional Certified Coach Level
   - Provider: Center for Executive Coaching (online and in-person blended)
   - Dates: 2014-03-01 to 2014-11-30
   - Duration: Approximately 150 coach-specific training hours over 9 months
   - Level: Aligned with ICF PCC-level competencies
   - Focus: Core coaching competencies, ethics, contracting, goal setting, feedback, and reflective practice. Included observed coaching sessions with written and verbal feedback, and a final performance evaluation.

2. MSc in Organizational Psychology
   - Institution: Portland State University
   - Dates: 2010-09-01 to 2012-06-15
   - Duration: 2-year full-time master’s degree
   - Level: Master’s degree (graduate level)
   - Relevant Content: Group dynamics, systems thinking, organizational development, change management, leadership, and team effectiveness. My thesis explored psychological safety and performance in cross-functional product teams.

3. EMCC Senior Practitioner Coach Certification
   - Accrediting Body: European Mentoring and Coaching Council (EMCC)
   - Date Awarded: 2018-05-10
   - Duration: Portfolio-based accreditation process over approximately 8 months, including supervised coaching practice and reflective log (over 100 hours of CPD documented)
   - Level: Senior Practitioner
   - Focus: Demonstrated advanced coaching competence, ethical practice, and ongoing supervision. Required evidence of over 500 hours of coaching, regular supervision, and client feedback.

4. Team Coaching Intensive – Systems-Approach Certification
   - Provider: Team Coaching International
   - Dates: 2017-02-10 to 2017-06-25
   - Duration: 12 days of training spread over 4 modules, plus practicum (approx. 90 training hours)
   - Level: Certificate in Team Coaching (intermediate to advanced)
   - Focus: Systems theory applied to teams, working with live teams, contracting with multiple stakeholders, team assessment tools, and co-facilitation. Completed a supervised practicum with a product development team in a mid-sized tech company, including 6 sessions of team coaching.

5. Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision
   - Provider: Coach Supervision Academy (CSA), London (blended online and residential modules)
   - Dates: 2019-01-15 to 2019-12-10
   - Duration: Approx. 150 hours of training plus 40 hours of supervised supervision practice
   - Level: Advanced Diploma (postgraduate-level professional qualification)
   - Focus: Integrative supervision models (Hawkins’ 7-Eyed Model, Proctor’s functions, and systemic supervision), ethics in supervision, parallel process, working with teams and groups in supervision, and the use of self as instrument. I completed a written dissertation on “Using systemic supervision to support internal team coaches in agile environments.”

6. EMCC Individual Coach Supervisor Accreditation
   - Accrediting Body: European Mentoring and Coaching Council (EMCC)
   - Date Awarded: 2021-03-18
   - Duration: 6-month evidence-based accreditation process
   - Level: Accredited Coach Supervisor
   - Focus: Demonstrated competence in contracting, ethical oversight, reflective practice, and working with complexity in supervisory relationships. Included client case studies, a reflective log, and supervisor-of-supervision reports.

7. Certificate in Group Facilitation & Process Consulting
   - Provider: National Training Laboratories (NTL Institute)
   - Dates: 2015-06-05 to 2015-10-20
   - Duration: 8 days of residential training plus peer practice groups (approx. 60 hours)
   - Level: Certificate
   - Focus: Group dynamics, process consultation, conflict facilitation, and creating psychologically safe learning environments. This training significantly shaped my comfort and skill in working with intact leadership teams.

8. Ongoing CPD (2016–2025)
   - Approx. 20–30 hours per year of continuing professional development
   - Examples:
     • 2020-09-12: 1-day online workshop on “Team Coaching in Virtual and Hybrid Environments” (8 hours)
     • 2022-04-03 to 2022-04-04: 2-day masterclass with Peter Hawkins on “Systemic Team Coaching and Supervision” (16 hours)
     • 2023-11-09: 1-day ethics in coaching and supervision refresher course (7 hours)
   - Focus: Keeping my skills current with emerging research on team effectiveness, hybrid teams, and ethical practice in supervision.

9. Internal Coach & Supervisor Training (Corporate Context)
   - Employer: Cascadia Health Systems (internal OD & leadership team)
   - Dates: 2012-09-01 to 2014-02-28
   - Duration: Internal coach training program of approx. 80 hours, including 6 months of mentored practice
   - Level: Internal coach certification within the organization
   - Focus: Leadership coaching skills, feedback conversations, and supporting newly formed cross-functional teams during a major electronic health record implementation.

Overall, my formal qualifications and certifications reflect a steady progression from individual coaching, to team coaching, and then into supervision, with a strong emphasis on systemic and organizational perspectives.

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach A Coach
answers.value:
I began practicing as an individual coach in late 2012, initially as part of my role in the Organizational Development team at Cascadia Health Systems. As of 2025, that gives me just over 12 years of coaching experience.

More specifically:
- 2012-2014: Primarily internal leadership and transition coaching (approx. 5–10 coaching hours per month while also working full-time in OD).
- 2014-2017: Transitioned to part-time independent practice alongside a half-time OD role, averaging 15–20 coaching hours per month.
- 2017-present: Full-time independent executive and leadership coach, typically averaging 35–45 coaching hours per month, depending on project load.

Across that time, I have accumulated approximately 1,800–2,000 hours of one-to-one coaching with clients ranging from frontline managers to C-suite leaders, primarily in healthcare, technology, and professional services. My focus has consistently been on leadership transitions, developing coaching cultures, and strengthening relational and emotional intelligence in leaders.

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach Team Coach
answers.value:
I started engaging in team coaching in an exploratory way around 2015, initially co-facilitating with a more experienced team coach during my group facilitation training. I count 2016 as the year I formally began practicing as a team coach with full responsibility for design and delivery.

Timeline and experience summary:
- 2015-2016: Co-facilitated 3 team development engagements as a junior partner (primarily leadership teams in healthcare and a non-profit board). These were a mix of workshops and emerging team coaching elements.
- 2016-2018: Led my own small-scale team coaching engagements (typically 4–6 sessions over 6–9 months) with cross-functional project teams in tech and healthcare. Averaged 4–6 team clients per year.
- 2018-present: Team coaching has become a central part of my practice. I typically work with 6–10 teams per year, often leadership or senior project teams, in engagements that range from 6–12 sessions over 9–18 months.

In total, I have approximately 9 years of experience in team coaching, with roughly 80–90 distinct team coaching cycles completed. My work has included:
- Coaching an executive leadership team of a regional hospital through a merger and integration over 14 months.
- Supporting a product leadership team in a mid-sized SaaS company as they moved to a scaled agile model, focusing on cross-team collaboration and conflict resolution.
- Working with a distributed, hybrid marketing team across multiple time zones to clarify purpose, roles, and ways of working post-pandemic.

My approach has evolved from a more workshop-oriented style to a systemic team coaching approach, where I pay careful attention to stakeholders, organizational context, and the broader system in which the team operates.

answers.field_label: How Long Have You Been Practicing As A Coach Coach Supervisor
answers.value:
I began practicing as a coach supervisor in a structured, formal way in early 2019, as part of my Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision. Prior to that, I occasionally offered informal peer supervision in my local coaching network, but I do not count that as formal supervisory practice.

Detailed timeline:
- 2019-01 to 2019-12: Supervision practicum during my Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision. I provided individual supervision to 6 coaches (a mix of internal and independent practitioners) and facilitated 2 small supervision groups. Approximately 45 hours of supervised supervision practice.
- 2020-2021: Continued to build my supervision practice, primarily offering individual supervision to 10–12 coaches per year and a regular monthly group supervision circle for internal coaches within a regional healthcare system (4–6 participants at a time).
- 2021-03: Achieved EMCC Coach Supervisor accreditation, which formally acknowledged my level of competence and experience.
- 2021-present: Supervision has become a significant part of my portfolio. I currently supervise:
  • Around 14 individual coaches on a regular 6–8 week cycle.
  • One ongoing group supervision cohort for internal team coaches in a global engineering firm (6–8 participants, meeting monthly).
  • Ad hoc supervision for coaches working in complex trauma-informed and healthcare environments.

In total, I have been practicing as a coach supervisor for approximately 6 years. I estimate I have delivered around 350–400 hours of formal supervision to date. Within that, a growing proportion (since 2022) has been specifically focused on supervising coaches who work with teams, including helping them navigate complexity, multiple contracting layers, ethical questions around confidentiality, and managing their own emotional responses to team dynamics.

I use a blend of systemic and relational approaches to supervision, drawing heavily on Hawkins’ 7-Eyed Model, reflective practice, and the idea of supervision as a collaborative learning partnership, where we are both continually making sense of what is happening in and around the coaching relationship.

answers.value:
In addition to individual coaching, team coaching, and supervision, I have been involved in several related roles:

1. Mentor Coach for Credentialing (ICF-oriented mentor coaching)
   - Duration: Since 2017 (approximately 8 years)
   - Description: I support coaches who are working towards ICF ACC and PCC credentials, focusing on competency development and reflective practice. Typically, I mentor 4–6 coaches per year in structured 3–6 month programs, including observed sessions and feedback. This role overlaps with supervision but is more explicitly competency-focused and developmental.

2. Internal Leadership Development Facilitator
   - Duration: 2012-2016 (4 years, overlapping with early coaching work)
   - Description: In my role at Cascadia Health Systems, I designed and facilitated leadership development programs that integrated coaching elements, such as peer coaching circles and action learning sets. While not labelled as “coaching,” much of the work involved coaching leaders in real time during workshops, debriefing their experiments, and helping them integrate learning into their daily work.

3. Group Coaching for Emerging Leaders
   - Duration: 2016-present (approx. 9 years)
   - Description: I run periodic group coaching cohorts (6–8 participants per group) for emerging leaders in small and mid-sized organizations. These groups usually run for 6–9 months with monthly 2-hour sessions, focusing on leadership identity, influence, and resilience. The format sits between team coaching and individual coaching, as the participants are not an intact team but share common themes and organizational contexts.

4. Coach Educator / Adjunct Faculty
   - Duration: 2020-present (approx. 5 years)
   - Description: I serve as adjunct faculty in a graduate-level leadership coaching certificate program at a local university. I teach a module on “Coaching in Organizations and Systems,” which includes an introduction to team coaching concepts and ethical considerations. I also offer supervision-like support for students as they complete their practicum.

Across these related roles, I continue to draw on and refine my coaching and supervision skills, particularly in creating reflective spaces, helping practitioners work with ambiguity and complexity, and supporting them to maintain ethical, sustainable practices. These experiences strongly inform my interest in further developing my capability specifically in team coaching supervision, as I increasingly support coaches who are working with complex, multi-stakeholder team environments.

persona.background_context:
This is Melissa, a mid-career executive and team coach who has spent the last decade steadily building a reputation in Portland and the wider Pacific Northwest as the person organizations call when their leadership teams are stuck, overwhelmed, or heading into big change. She runs her own practice, Cartwright Leadership & Team Coaching, LLC, out of a light-filled shared office space downtown on Willow Creek Lane, a short bike ride from the family’s Craftsman-style house in Southeast Portland’s Richmond neighborhood. Her week is a carefully balanced patchwork of one-to-one executive coaching sessions over Zoom, longer in-person team coaching days at client sites, and supervision sessions with other coaches—many of whom are themselves learning to work with complex, hybrid teams. Two mornings a week she also teaches as adjunct faculty in a graduate-level leadership coaching certificate program at Portland State University, where her module on coaching in organizations and systems has become known for its practical case studies and frank discussions about ethical grey areas.

Life at home is busy but mostly warm and grounded. Melissa and her husband, Daniel, have been married for 13 years and are firmly in the “dual-career, school-age kids” phase of life. Ellie, their 10-year-old, is deeply into soccer and graphic novels, while 7-year-old Jake is the kind of kid who wants to take everything apart to see how it works—recently, this included an old Wi-Fi router that Melissa forgot to move out of reach. Weekdays start early: Melissa usually gets up around 5:45 a.m. for a short yoga practice in the living room and a quick skim of overnight emails before the kids tumble downstairs. Mornings are a choreography of packing lunches, locating missing shin guards, and negotiating who gets the bathroom first. On Mondays and Thursdays, Daniel does school drop-off so Melissa can be at her desk by 8:00 a.m. for supervision calls with clients on East Coast time. On other days, she walks the kids to school, using the 15-minute walk as a mental reset before heading either to her downtown office or back to her home workspace overlooking their small backyard garden.

Over the past few years, Melissa’s work has shifted from mostly individual executive coaching to a portfolio where team coaching and supervision take center stage. She currently has a long-running engagement with the executive team of a regional hospital system navigating post-pandemic staffing challenges and a complex merger, as well as a hybrid product leadership team in a mid-sized SaaS company that’s trying to move to a more truly cross-functional way of working. These engagements are intellectually stimulating but also emotionally demanding, and she’s learned to protect Fridays as her “thinking and admin” days: no more than one client meeting, time blocked out for reflection notes, supervision of her own supervision practice, and planning for the next cycle of group coaching for emerging leaders. She’s been tracking her working hours more closely after realizing last year that she’d drifted up to 50–55-hour weeks. One of her quiet goals for 2025 is to stay closer to 40–45 hours without compromising the depth of her work.

Recent months have brought a mix of personal and professional shifts. Her mother, Maggie, was diagnosed with early-stage breast cancer last fall, and although the prognosis is good, the treatment schedule has meant more frequent weekend trips down to Eugene to help with appointments and household logistics. Melissa and her younger sister, Hannah, coordinate via a shared calendar and a long-running text thread that swings easily between medical updates, kid photos, and dark humor. These family responsibilities sometimes mean rescheduling non-urgent client work, and Melissa has found herself talking more openly with her supervisees about sustainability, boundaries, and the reality that coaches are human beings with complex lives, too.

At the same time, her business is in a growth phase. Word-of-mouth referrals from past clients and her EMCC supervision accreditation have led to a waiting list for individual supervision. She’s experimenting with a new group supervision cohort specifically for team coaches working in healthcare and tech, planning to launch the next group in September. She’s also been invited to co-facilitate a two-day systemic team coaching masterclass with a colleague she admires, which will require a bit of travel to Seattle and some careful childcare coordination. Melissa enjoys this blend of local and regional work; it keeps her connected to different organizational cultures while still allowing her to be home most evenings for family dinner, which she and Daniel guard fiercely as an anchor in the week.

Outside of work, Melissa finds refuge in small, consistent practices rather than big, dramatic hobbies. She’s part of a neighborhood book club that meets once a month at a nearby wine bar, though she doesn’t always finish the books on time. On Sunday afternoons, weather permitting, the family often bikes along the Springwater Corridor Trail, stopping at a food cart pod on the way home. She keeps a modest vegetable garden—kale, cherry tomatoes, herbs—and jokes with clients that it’s her most honest feedback system: if she neglects it, the plants let her know. She’s also slowly rehabbing a nagging shoulder issue from years of hunching over a laptop, working with a physical therapist and trying to take her own advice about pacing and listening to her body.

Underneath the busyness, Melissa is quietly wrestling with questions about what the next decade of her professional life should look like. Part of her is drawn to writing more—perhaps a book on systemic supervision in team coaching or a series of articles based on the dissertation she wrote in 2019 about supporting internal team coaches in agile environments. Another part worries about overextending herself and losing the intimacy she values in one-to-one and small-group work. For now, she’s experimenting in small steps: a quarterly reflective newsletter to her network, guest lectures at the university beyond her usual module, and a commitment to attend at least one high-quality CPD event each year that stretches her thinking. Her days are full, but not in a way that feels random; they’re the product of many deliberate choices, regularly revisited in the same reflective, systemic way she encourages in the leaders and coaches she supports.

secrets.detail_content: In 2019, alongside my Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision with Coach Supervision Academy (CSA) in London (2019-01-15 to 2019-12-10, approx. 150 hours plus 40 hours of supervised supervision practice, postgraduate level), I completed an additional 12 hours of one-to-one reflective supervision with Dr. Karen Lewis, a Jungian analyst and coach supervisor based at the Portland Jungian Clinic, specifically to process the vicarious trauma I was carrying from supervising an internal coaching cohort at Cascadia Health Systems during a period when one of the senior physicians on their executive team died by suicide in June 2019.
secrets.why_sensitive: While the diploma and hours are relevant, disclosing that the reflective supervision addressed vicarious trauma related to a physician suicide at a named employer and clinic reveals sensitive mental health, workplace crisis, and third-party death information that is unnecessary for admissions and could create bias or violate others’ privacy.

artifacts.content:
Hi Hannah,

I’m hiding in the Willow Creek office between Zooms and thought I’d actually write instead of sending another chaotic text thread.

First, Dad: how is he really doing this week? I keep thinking about Sacred Heart after his stroke in 2011 and then the ladder fall in 2021, and how every time we think we’re through the worst, something else pops up. I’ve blocked off the first weekend in April to come down to Eugene so you’re not carrying all of Maggie’s oncology appointments alone. Can we sync around her next round at the cancer center and who’s taking which Sacred Heart runs for Bob’s cardiology follow‑ups?

Work is its usual “nothing… and everything” at once. I’m still holding that long-running exec team at the regional hospital, plus the hybrid SaaS product leadership team downtown. On top of that I’ve got a supervision case from Cascadia Health Systems rattling around in my head again – remember when I did that informal, pro bono supervision practicum there from September 2016 to May 2017? Five internal coaches, about 25 hours of group supervision and 15 hours individually, all because of that ugly wrongful‑termination lawsuit in October 2016 about systemic bullying in the ICU. Three of them ended up on stress‑related medical leave. I can still picture that OD office in Portland and the whiteboard full of “safe escalation routes” we drew.

Some days it feels like my whole career is just walking into other people’s crises and trying to help them see the system around them. Then I come home and it’s spelling lists, Jake’s science project, and Ellie’s soccer carpools.

Speaking of, can you send me Oliver’s and Lucy’s spring break dates? The kids keep asking when they’ll see their cousins.

Love,
M

Hi Laura,

I’ve just made myself a second cup of coffee and wanted to say a proper thank‑you after the ethics masterclass last month.

The timing could not have been more on‑the‑nose. In February, one of my supervisees – an internal team coach at the health tech company where my husband Daniel is a senior product manager – disclosed that he’d cornered her after a retro and “just casually” asked what had come up in a confidential team coaching session about a product VP’s drinking problem and those repeated Monday‑morning hangovers. It hit every single conflict‑of‑interest and boundary nerve I have. We did the right things in the moment (she didn’t share anything, we re‑contracted, I named the dual relationship), but it left a residue.

Your 6‑hour online ethics in supervision masterclass on Zoom in April really helped me sort through what belongs in supervision, what belongs in my own therapy (Dr. Mills at Providence is getting an earful), and what needs to be a very boring, grown‑up conversation with Daniel about lines he cannot cross as long as I’m supervising anyone in that building downtown.

On a lighter note, I’m trying to keep Fridays as “thinking/admin” days again – only one client, then supervision notes, CPD planning, and maybe actually watering the tomatoes before they wilt. I’m also sketching ideas for that group supervision cohort for team coaches in healthcare/tech I mentioned; your piece on power and proximity is going to be required reading.

Hope you’re getting at least glimpses of spring wherever you are.

Warmly,
Melissa

Supervision‑related training that actually shaped my practice:

– 2019 Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision with Coach Supervision Academy (London, blended): 150+ hours plus 40 hours of supervised supervision practice. Alongside the diploma I did those extra 12 hours of one‑to‑one reflective supervision with Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland Jungian Clinic to process the vicarious trauma from supervising the internal coaching cohort at Cascadia Health Systems after the senior physician on their executive team died by suicide in June 2019. – 2020 Spring: 8‑week supervision skills booster (~24 hours) with Helena Morris through Pacific Northwest Coaching Collective – I signed up after my long‑term executive client at that Portland health tech startup started disclosing active opioid misuse and suicidal ideation in our March 2020 Zooms, right as the first COVID lockdowns hit. Earlier stuff:

Hi Janet,

I’m doing my annual “how did another year of supervision happen?” reflection and thought you’d appreciate a candid download.

Formally I’ve been supervising since January 2019, starting with the Advanced Diploma practicum, so we’re at about six years now. The hours add up – somewhere around 350–400 of formal supervision delivered – but they’ve come in waves. Remember that 30‑hour gap in my log from June through August 2020? That was when Abernethy Elementary shut down after the COVID outbreak in Ellie’s 5th‑grade class and Jake’s 2nd‑grade pod. I was suddenly home‑schooling long division and Oregon Trail projects instead of running group supervision.

The supervision‑of‑supervision with Alan Hughes from October 2021 to March 2022 was a lifesaver. Eighteen hours with that Exeter group, and we spent a good chunk of it on the Cascadia Health Systems case where my supervisee – an internal coach – was under an informal HR investigation for allegedly breaching confidentiality. She’d mentioned a VP’s panic attacks and Xanax use in a team coaching debrief in November 2021. Untangling my contracting there made me much braver about naming limits up front.

On the personal side, Daniel and I are mostly okay after the 2020 “surprise” credit card saga, the kids are thriving (Ellie is deep into soccer; Jake dismantled an old router yesterday), and I’m still guarding Fridays as thinking/admin days.

I’m also noticing a two‑year lull after the CSA diploma where I didn’t do much formal supervision‑specific CPD – I focused on business development and just keeping the wheels on through the pandemic. Not ideal, but honest.

If you have any thoughts on what would stretch me next, I’d love to hear them.

With gratitude,
Melissa

Hi Karen,

I’m holed up in the downtown office between PSU marking and a supervision group and suddenly realised I haven’t properly updated you in ages.

Work first: team coaching has fully taken over my life in the best and worst ways. I still think of those three co‑facilitations we did together at OHSU in late 2015 – me just back from postpartum leave after Jake, trying not to cry in the elevator – as the real beginning.
